---
title: "Exercises: Subspaces, Basis, and Dimension"
description: "Practice problems and worked solutions for subspace checks, basis construction, and dimension counting."
page-type: exercise
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: linear-algebra
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/subspaces-basis-dimension.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Check whether a set is a subspace."
  - "Build a basis from a spanning set."
  - "Interpret dimension as number of independent directions."
applications:
  - regression
  - dimensionality-reduction
keywords:
  - exercises
  - subspace
  - basis
  - dimension
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/proofs/exchange-argument-and-dimension.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/labs/basis-and-column-space-geometry.qmd
---

## Scope and Goals

This set trains four things:

- checking subspace conditions carefully
- extracting a basis from a spanning description
- counting dimension correctly
- connecting column space language to later least-squares geometry

## Prerequisites

- span and linear combinations
- independence
- column space as a set of outputs $Ax$

## Warm-Up Problems

1. Decide whether

   $$
   \left\{
   \begin{bmatrix}
   x \\
   y \\
   z
   \end{bmatrix}
   : x+y+z=0
   \right\}
   $$

   is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$.
2. Find a basis for the line

   $$
   \operatorname{span}\left\{
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 \\
   -1
   \end{bmatrix}
   \right\}.
   $$

## Core Problems

1. Find a basis and the dimension of

   $$
   W =
   \operatorname{span}
   \left\{
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   0 \\
   1
   \end{bmatrix},
   \begin{bmatrix}
   0 \\
   1 \\
   1
   \end{bmatrix}
   \right\}.
   $$

2. Explain why

   $$
   \left\{
   \begin{bmatrix}
   x \\
   y
   \end{bmatrix}
   : x+y=1
   \right\}
   $$

   is not a subspace.
3. Let

   $$
   A =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 & 0 & 1 \\
   0 & 1 & 1
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

   Describe a basis for $\operatorname{col}(A)$.

## Proof Problems

1. Show that the column space of any matrix is a subspace.
2. Prove that removing a redundant vector from a spanning set does not change the span.

## Computational or Applied Problems

1. Open [Computation Lab: Basis and Column Space Geometry](../labs/basis-and-column-space-geometry.qmd). Change the angle and explain when the third vector changes the subspace and when it does not.
2. In software, create a matrix with three columns in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and compare the number of columns with the actual dimension of the column space.

## Hints

1. A subspace must contain the zero vector.
2. To test independence, solve $a_1v_1 + a_2v_2 = 0$.
3. Different spanning sets can define the same subspace.

## Full Solutions

### Solution 1

Yes. The set contains $0$, and if $x+y+z=0$ and $x'+y'+z'=0$, then

$$
(x+x') + (y+y') + (z+z') = 0.
$$

Scalar multiples also preserve the equation, so the set is a subspace.

### Solution 2

A basis is just

$$
\left\{
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\
-1
\end{bmatrix}
\right\}.
$$

The dimension is $1$.

### Solution 3

The two given vectors are independent, so they already form a basis. Therefore

$$
\dim(W)=2.
$$

### Solution 4

The vector $(0,0)^\top$ is not in the set because $0+0 \neq 1$. So the set cannot be a subspace.

### Solution 5

The columns are

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

The first two already span $\mathbb{R}^2$, and the third is their sum. So a basis is the first two columns.

## Common Errors

- forgetting that a subspace must contain zero
- confusing ambient dimension with subspace dimension
- thinking every spanning set is already a basis

## What To Do Next

Open [Orthogonality and Least Squares](../concepts/orthogonality-and-least-squares.qmd) next. That page turns column space into the target subspace for approximation.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.06SC: Basis and Dimension](https://live.ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011/resources/basis-and-dimension-1/) - `First pass` - official exercise context for span, basis, and dimension. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Hefferon, Linear Algebra](https://hefferon.net/linearalgebra/) - `Second pass` - stronger self-study exercise source on subspaces and bases. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [A Survey: Potential Dimensionality Reduction Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.11036) - `Paper bridge` - later reminder that low-dimensional models need honest basis and dimension reasoning. Checked `2026-04-24`.
